name: Manikyanandi
dates_notes: Māṇikyanandi is generally placed around the eighth to ninth century from his position after Akalaṅka and before Prabhācandra's commentary tradition.
core_thesis: Māṇikyanandi is the Digambara Jaina author of the *Parīkṣāmukha*, a concise sūtra-style entrance text for Jaina logic. The work gives a compact statement of the Jaina theory of valid cognition, inference, and standpoint-governed predication, and it became important because Prabhācandra's *Prameya-Kamala-Mārtaṇḍa* made it the base for a fuller scholastic commentary. The doctrinal content should be stated at this level: Māṇikyanandi condenses the mature Digambara pramāṇa tradition after Akalaṅka into a teaching text, framing knowledge and debate through a realist but many-sided account of objects. A readable archive OCR of a modern non-copyright edition/translation has been added, but the Sanskrit OCR is not citation-grade.

engaged_works.title: Pariksamukha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The work is standardly transmitted as Māṇikyanandi's Jaina logic manual and is the base text for Prabhācandra's commentary.
engaged_works.summary: A concise Digambara Jaina manual of logic and epistemology. The text introduces the marks of valid cognition, the structure of inferential reasoning, and the role of standpoint-conditioned predication in Jaina debate. Its form is compact enough to function as a teaching text, which explains why later commentators, especially Prabhācandra, use it as the basis for more extensive scholastic argument. The local OCR source is a modern edition/translation from Archive.org and is useful for orientation, but it is not clean enough for exact Sanskrit citation without correction.
